title: Sarga 34
summary: Rama's divine birth, childhood, protection of Sage Vishwamitra's rituals from demons, breaking of Shiva's bow, and marriage to Sita. Addresses youth, childhood, education, overcoming early obstacles, and marriage.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Indeed, Ravana, the lord of Rakshasas, having been released by Arjuna and treated in such a manner, roamed all over the earth undeterred.” and develops toward “There, Ravana saw Valin, who resembled a golden mountain with a face like the rising sun, intently engaged in his twilight prayers.”

This passage begins with “Then, Ravana, dark like collyrium, descended from the Pushpaka chariot and quickly approached Valin with silent steps, intending to seize him.” and develops toward “Vali, being followed by those Rakshasas in the middle of the sky, shone like the sun in the sky being followed by masses of clouds.”

This passage begins with “Those chief Rakshasas, unable to reach Vali due to his immense speed, became exhausted and stopped.” and develops toward “Ravana addressed Bali, saying, "O king of monkeys, resembling Mahendra, I am Ravana, the king of Rakshasas, who arrived here desirous of battle, and…”

This passage begins with “Ravana exclaimed, "Oh, what strength, what valor, and what profundity, by which I was seized like an animal and carried around all four oceans!"” and develops toward “O Rama, Vali possessed supreme and unequalled strength, yet even he was completely destroyed by you, just as a moth is consumed by fire. इत्यार्षे…”

verses.sanskrit: अर्जुनेन विमुक्तस्तु रावणो राक्षसाधिपः । चचार पृथिवीं सर्वामनिर्विण्णस्तथा कृतः ।। 7.34.1 ।।
verses.translation: Indeed, Ravana, the lord of Rakshasas, having been released by Arjuna and treated in such a manner, roamed all over the earth undeterred.

verses.sanskrit: राक्षसं वा मनुष्यं वा शृणुते ऽयं बलाधिकम् । रावणस्तं समासाद्य युद्धे ह्वयति दर्पितः ।। 7.34.2 ।।
verses.translation: This arrogant Ravana, upon hearing of any Rakshasa or human superior in strength, would approach them and challenge them to battle.

verses.sanskrit: ततः कदाचित्किष्किन्धां नगरीं वालिपालिताम् । गत्वा ह्वयति युद्धाय वालिनं हेममालिनम् ।। 7.34.3 ।।
verses.translation: Then, once, having gone to the city of Kishkindha, which was protected by Vali, he challenged Vali, who was adorned with a golden garland, for battle.

verses.sanskrit: ततस्तु वानरामात्यस्तारस्तारापिता प्रभुः । उवाच वानरो वाक्यं युद्धप्रेप्सुमुपागतम् ।। 7.34.4 ।।
verses.translation: But then, Sushena, the powerful minister of the monkeys and father of Tara, spoke words to the one who had come near, desirous of battle.

verses.sanskrit: राक्षसेन्द गतो वाली यस्ते प्रतिबलो भवेत् । को ऽन्यः प्रमुखतः स्थातुं तव शक्तः प्लवङ्गमः ।। 7.34.5 ।।
verses.translation: O king of Rakshasas, Vali, who would be an equal opponent to you, has departed; what other monkey is capable of standing before you?

verses.sanskrit: चतुर्भ्यो ऽपि समुद्रेभ्यः सन्ध्यामन्वास्य रावण । इमं मुहूर्तमायाति वाली तिष्ठ मुहूर्तकम् ।। 7.34.6 ।।
verses.translation: O Ravana, Vali is returning in a moment, having performed his twilight prayers from all four oceans; wait for just a moment.

verses.sanskrit: एतानस्थिचयान्पश्य य एते शङ्खपाण्डुराः । युद्धार्थिनामिमे राजन्वानराधिपतेजसा ।। 7.34.7 ।।
verses.translation: O king, behold these conch-white piles of bones, which are the remains of those who desired battle, vanquished by the might of the king of monkeys.

verses.sanskrit: यद्वा ऽमृतरसः पीतस्त्वया रावण राक्षस । तदा वालिनमासाद्य तदन्तं तव जीवितम् ।। 7.34.8 ।।
verses.translation: O demon Ravana, even if you have drunk the nectar of immortality, your life will surely end once you encounter Valin.

verses.sanskrit: पश्येदानीं जगच्चित्रमिमं विश्रवसः सुत । इदं मुहूर्तं तिष्ठस्व दुर्लभं ते भविष्यति ।। 7.34.9 ।।
verses.translation: O son of Vishravas, behold this wonder of the world now, and wait for a moment, for such an opportunity will be rare for you in the future.

verses.sanskrit: अथवा त्वरसे मर्तुं गच्छ दक्षिणसागरम् । वालिनं द्रक्ष्यसे तत्र भूमिस्थमिव पावकम् ।। 7.34.10 ।।
verses.translation: Alternatively, if you are eager to die, go to the southern ocean, where you will see Valin, who is like a fire standing on the earth.

verses.sanskrit: स तु तारं विनिर्भर्त्स्य रावणो लोकरावणः । पुष्पकं तत्समारुह्य प्रययौ दक्षिणार्णवम् ।। 7.34.11 ।।
verses.translation: Indeed, that Ravana, the tormentor of the worlds, having severely rebuked Tara, ascended his Pushpaka chariot and set off towards the southern ocean.

verses.sanskrit: तत्र हेमगिरिप्रख्यं तरुणार्कनिभाननम् । रावणो वालिनं दृष्ट्वा सन्ध्योपासनतत्परम् ।। 7.34.12 ।।
verses.translation: There, Ravana saw Valin, who resembled a golden mountain with a face like the rising sun, intently engaged in his twilight prayers.

verses.sanskrit: पुष्पकादवरुह्याथ रावणो ऽञ्जनसन्निभः । ग्रहीतुं वालिनं तूर्णं निःशब्दपदमाव्रजत् ।। 7.34.13 ।।
verses.translation: Then, Ravana, dark like collyrium, descended from the Pushpaka chariot and quickly approached Valin with silent steps, intending to seize him.

verses.sanskrit: यदृच्छया तदा दृष्टो वालिनापि स रावणः । पापाभिप्रायवान् दृष्ट्वा चकार न तु सम्भ्रमम् ।। 7.34.14 ।।
verses.translation: By chance, Valin then saw that Ravana, who had evil intentions, but upon seeing him, Valin showed no agitation.

verses.sanskrit: शशमालक्ष्य सिंहो वा पन्नगं गरुडो यथा । न चिन्तयति तं वाली रावणं पापनिश्चयम् ।। 7.34.15 ।।
verses.translation: Just as a lion does not worry about a rabbit, or Garuda about a serpent, so Valin did not consider that evil-minded Ravana a threat.

verses.sanskrit: जिघृक्षमाणमायान्तं रावणं पापचेतसम् । कक्षावलम्बिनं कृत्वा गमिष्ये त्रीन्महार्णवान् ।। 7.34.16 ।।
verses.translation: Valin thought, "I will make this evil-minded Ravana, who is coming to seize me, hang from my armpit and then I shall traverse the three great oceans."

verses.sanskrit: द्रक्ष्यन्त्यरिं ममाङ्कस्थं स्रंसदूरुकराम्बरम् । लम्बमानं दशग्रीवं गरुडस्येव पन्नगम् ।। 7.34.17 ।।
verses.translation: Valin further thought, "They will see my enemy Dashagriva, with his thighs, hands, and clothes slipping, hanging from my side like a serpent from Garuda."

verses.sanskrit: इत्येवं मतिमास्थाय वाली कर्णमुपाश्रितः । जपन्वै नैगमान्मन्त्रांस्तस्थौ पर्वतराडिव ।। 7.34.18 ।।
verses.translation: Vali, having adopted this resolve, resorted to a secluded spot and stood there, chanting Vedic mantras, appearing like the king of mountains.

verses.sanskrit: तावन्योन्यं जिघृक्षन्तौ हरिराक्षसपार्थिवौ । प्रयत्नवन्तौ तत्कर्म ईहतुर्बलदर्पितौ ।। 7.34.19 ।।
verses.translation: Those two kings, Vali and Ravana, both proud of their strength and desiring to seize each other, engaged in that struggle with great effort.

verses.sanskrit: हस्तग्राहं तु तं मत्वा पादशब्देन रावणम् । पराङ्मुखो ऽपि जग्राह वाली सर्पमिवाण्डजः ।। 7.34.20 ।।
verses.translation: Vali, perceiving Ravana by the sound of his footsteps and considering him within reach, seized him even while facing away, just as a bird seizes a snake.

verses.sanskrit: ग्रहीतुकामं तं गृह्य रक्षसामीश्वरं हरिः । खमुत्पपात वेगेन कृत्वा कक्षावलम्बिनम् ।। 7.34.21 ।।
verses.translation: The monkey king Vali, having seized Ravana, the lord of demons, who had intended to capture him, swiftly flew up into the sky, holding Ravana hanging from his armpit.

verses.sanskrit: तं चापीडयमानं तु वितुदन्तं नखैर्मुहुः । जहार रावणं वाली पवनस्तोयदं यथा ।। 7.34.22 ।।
verses.translation: Vali carried away Ravana, who was being oppressed and repeatedly scratching him with his nails, just as the wind carries away a cloud.

verses.sanskrit: अथ ते राक्षसामात्या ह्रियमाणं दशाननम् । मुमोक्षयिषवो वालिं रवमाणा अभिद्रुताः ।। 7.34.23 ।।
verses.translation: Then, those Rakshasa ministers, seeing Dashanana being carried away by Vali, rushed after him, shouting, desiring to free their king.

verses.sanskrit: अन्वीयमानस्तैर्वाली भ्राजते ऽम्बरमध्यगः । अन्वीयमानो मेघौघैरम्बरस्थ इवांशुमान् ।। 7.34.24 ।।
verses.translation: Vali, being followed by those Rakshasas in the middle of the sky, shone like the sun in the sky being followed by masses of clouds.

verses.sanskrit: ते ऽशक्नुवन्तः सम्प्राप्तुं वालिनं राक्षसोत्तमाः । तस्य बाहूरुवेगेन परिश्रान्ता व्यवस्थिताः ।। 7.34.25 ।।
verses.translation: Those chief Rakshasas, unable to reach Vali due to his immense speed, became exhausted and stopped.

verses.sanskrit: वालिमार्गादपाक्रामन्पर्वतेन्द्रो हि गच्छतः । किं पुनर्जीवितप्रेप्सुर्बिभ्रद्वै मांसशोणितम् ।। 7.34.26 ।।
verses.translation: Indeed, even a great mountain would move away from Vali's path as he flew, so how much more would those Rakshasas, who were mere beings of flesh and blood desiring to live, be unable to keep up.

verses.sanskrit: अपक्षिगणसम्पातान्वानरेन्द्रो महाजवः । क्रमशः सागरान्सर्वान्सन्ध्याकालमवन्दत ।। 7.34.27 ।।
verses.translation: The immensely swift king of monkeys, Vali, flew through regions where even flocks of birds could not reach, successively worshipping all the oceans as the twilight hour arrived.

verses.sanskrit: सभाज्यमानो भूतैस्तु खैचरैः खैचरोत्तमः । पश्चिमं सागरं वाली ह्याजगाम सरावणः ।। 7.34.28 ।।
verses.translation: Vali, the best among sky-dwellers, being honored by various celestial beings, indeed reached the western ocean with Ravana.

verses.sanskrit: तस्मिन्सन्ध्यामुपासित्वा स्नात्वा जप्त्वा च वानरः । उत्तरं सागरं प्रायाद्वहमानो दशाननम् ।। 7.34.29 ।।
verses.translation: Having performed his evening prayers, bathed, and chanted mantras there, Vali, the monkey, proceeded to the northern ocean, still carrying Ravana.

verses.sanskrit: बहुयोजनसाहस्रं वहमानो महाहरिः । वायुवच्च मनोवच्च जगाम सह शत्रुणा ।। 7.34.30 ।।
verses.translation: The great monkey, carrying his enemy for many thousands of yojanas, traveled with the speed of wind and thought.

verses.sanskrit: उत्तरे सागरे सन्ध्यामुपासित्वा दशाननम् । वहमानो ऽगमद्वाली पूर्वं वै स महोदधिम् ।। 7.34.31 ।।
verses.translation: After performing his evening prayers in the northern ocean, Vali, still carrying Ravana, then proceeded to the eastern great ocean.

verses.sanskrit: तत्रापि सन्ध्यामन्वास्य वासविः सहरीश्वरः । किष्किन्धामभितो गृह्य रावणं पुनरागमत् ।। 7.34.32 ।।
verses.translation: Having performed his evening prayers there as well, Vali, the lord of monkeys, returned towards Kishkindha, still holding Ravana.

verses.sanskrit: चतुर्ष्वपि समुद्रेषु सन्ध्यामन्वास्य वानरः । रावणोद्वहनश्रान्तः किष्किन्धोपवने ऽपतत् ।। 7.34.33 ।।
verses.translation: After performing his twilight prayers in all four oceans, the monkey (Bali), exhausted from carrying Ravana, landed in the pleasure grove of Kishkindha.

verses.sanskrit: रावणं तु मुमोचाथ स्वकक्षात्कपिसत्तमः । कुतस्त्वमिति चोवाच प्रहसन्रावणं मुहुः ।। 7.34.34 ।।
verses.translation: Then, the best of monkeys (Bali) released Ravana from his armpit and, laughing repeatedly, asked Ravana, "Where are you from?"

verses.sanskrit: विस्मयं तु महद्गत्वा श्रमलोलनिरीक्षणः । राक्षसेन्द्रो हरीन्द्रं तमिदं वचनमब्रवीत् ।। 7.34.35 ।।
verses.translation: Having experienced great astonishment and with his gaze unsteady from fatigue, the king of Rakshasas (Ravana) spoke these words to that king of monkeys (Bali).

verses.sanskrit: वानरेन्द्र महेन्द्राभ राक्षसेन्द्रो ऽस्मि रावणः । युद्धेप्सुरिह सम्प्राप्तः स चाद्यासादितस्त्वया ।। 7.34.36 ।।
verses.translation: Ravana addressed Bali, saying, "O king of monkeys, resembling Mahendra, I am Ravana, the king of Rakshasas, who arrived here desirous of battle, and that desire has been fulfilled by you today."

verses.sanskrit: अहो बलमहो वीर्यमहो गाम्भीर्यमेव च । येनाहं पशुवद्गृह्य भ्रामितश्चतुरो ऽर्णवान् ।। 7.34.37 ।।
verses.translation: Ravana exclaimed, "Oh, what strength, what valor, and what profundity, by which I was seized like an animal and carried around all four oceans!"

verses.sanskrit: एवमश्रान्तवद्वीर शीघ्रमेव महार्णवान् । मां चैवोद्वहमानस्तु को ऽन्यो वीरः क्रमिष्यति ।। 7.34.38 ।।
verses.translation: O hero, who else but you, carrying me and the great oceans, will swiftly and tirelessly traverse them?

verses.sanskrit: त्रयाणामेव भूतानां गतिरेषा प्लवङ्गम । मनोनिलसुपर्णानां तव चात्र न संशयः ।। 7.34.39 ।।
verses.translation: O monkey, this speed belongs to only three beings: the mind, the wind, and Suparna (Garuda), and there is no doubt that it also belongs to you.

verses.sanskrit: सो ऽहं दृष्टबलस्तुभ्यमिच्छामि हरिपुङ्गव । त्वया सह चिरं सख्यं सुस्निग्धं पावकाग्रतः ।। 7.34.40 ।।
verses.translation: O best of monkeys, having witnessed your strength, I desire to establish a very firm and lasting friendship with you in the presence of fire.

verses.sanskrit: दाराः पुत्राः पुरं राष्ट्रं भोगाच्छादनभोजनम् । सर्वमेवाविभक्तं नौ भविष्यति हरीश्वर ।। 7.34.41 ।।
verses.translation: O lord of monkeys, our wives, sons, city, kingdom, enjoyments, clothing, and food, all will indeed be undivided between us.

verses.sanskrit: ततः प्रज्वालयित्वाग्निं तावुभौ हरीराक्षसौ । भ्रातृत्वमुपसम्पन्नौ परिष्वज्य परस्परम् ।। 7.34.42 ।।
verses.translation: Then, having kindled a fire, both the monkey (Hanuman) and the Rakshasa (Vibhishana) established their brotherhood and embraced each other.

verses.sanskrit: अन्योन्यं लम्बितकरौ ततस्तौ हरिराक्षसौ । किष्किन्धां विशतुर्हृष्टौ सिंहौ गिरिगुहामिव ।। 7.34.43 ।।
verses.translation: Then, those two, the monkey (Vali) and the demon (Ravana), with hands clasped, joyfully entered Kishkindha, just like two lions entering a mountain cave.

verses.sanskrit: स तत्र मासमुषितः सुग्रीव इव रावणः । अमात्यैरागतैर्नीतस्त्रैलोक्योत्सादनार्थिभिः ।। 7.34.44 ।।
verses.translation: Ravana, having stayed there for a month like Sugriva, was then led away by his ministers who had arrived, desiring the destruction of the three worlds.

verses.sanskrit: एवमेतत्पुरावृत्तं वालिना रावणः प्रभो । धर्षितश्च कृतश्चापि भ्राता पावकसन्निधौ ।। 7.34.45 ।।
verses.translation: O Lord, thus did this happen formerly: Ravana was humiliated by Vali and also made his brother in the presence of fire.

verses.sanskrit: बलमप्रतिमं राम वालिनो ऽभवदुत्तमम् । सो ऽपि त्वया विनिर्दग्धः शलभो वह्निना यथा ।। 7.34.46 ।।
verses.translation: O Rama, Vali possessed supreme and unequalled strength, yet even he was completely destroyed by you, just as a moth is consumed by fire. इत्यार्षे श्रीमद्रामायणे वाल्मीकीये आदिकाव्ये श्रीमदुत्तरकाण्डे चतुस्त्रिंशः सर्गः ।। 34 ।। Thus ends the thirty-fourth sarga of Uttara Kanda of the holy Ramayana the first epic composed by sage Valmiki.
